title: A Year Later, Trump’s Big Beautiful Bill Act Has a Clean Tech Silver Lining | Council on Foreign Relations
gist: David Hart argues that despite eliminating IRA wind and solar tax credits, Trump's One Big Beautiful Bill Act (OB3) preserved credits for immature clean technologies — enhanced geothermal, advanced nuclear, and long-duration storage — which could do more for global emissions than the IRA by pushing these technologies down the cost 'learning curve,' with Fervo, Kairos, Oklo, X-Energy, Form Energy, and Antora Energy already showing capital-raising momentum.

themes.theme: A contrarian read on OB3 vs. the IRA

insights.claim: Despite climate campaigners calling Trump's One Big Beautiful Bill Act (OB3) a 'disaster,' the law could do more to advance global clean-energy progress than the Biden-era Inflation Reduction Act (IRA) it replaced, because OB3 preserved tax credits for immature clean technologies while the IRA's largest incentives targeted already-mature wind and solar.
insights.speaker: David M. Hart
insights.so_what: Reframes a widely-panned climate rollback as a targeted bet on emerging-technology learning curves, useful for weighing OB3's actual global emissions impact against its domestic political framing.

insights.claim: Wind and solar are mature technologies whose U.S. market expansion has virtually no effect on their global costs, since most future emissions growth comes from emerging markets, so eliminating their IRA tax credits 'would have hardly moved the world's climate needle' even though it raises domestic emissions.
insights.speaker: David M. Hart
insights.so_what: Argues the solar/wind subsidy repeal is largely climate-immaterial at the global level, a useful counterweight to headline climate-alarm framing when assessing OB3's real-world emissions trajectory.
insights.quote: would have hardly moved the world's climate needle
insights.locator: But wind and solar are mature technologies

themes.theme: The learning-curve bet on immature tech

insights.claim: OB3 preserved tax credits for enhanced geothermal power, advanced nuclear power, and long-duration energy storage on the theory that these technologies are costly now but could become much cheaper once scaled, with U.S.-funded early deployment covering the high front-end costs of the 'learning curve' so the rest of the world can afford them later — the same sequence solar power went through roughly a decade ago.
insights.speaker: David M. Hart
insights.so_what: Names the specific mechanism — front-loaded U.S. cost absorption driving global cost reduction — that could make OB3's narrow tech-specific credits more consequential for global emissions than the IRA's broad wind/solar credits.
insights.locator: The reason is that these technologies are costly now

insights.claim: Enhanced geothermal, advanced nuclear, and long-duration storage could together supply between 500 and 1,000 gigawatts of global electricity generation capacity by 2050, nearly as much as the entire U.S. grid built over the past 150 years.
insights.speaker: David M. Hart
insights.so_what: Sizes the addressable market these technologies could reach by mid-century, a scale marker for evaluating long-horizon investment theses in geothermal, advanced nuclear, and storage.
insights.locator: All of these technologies could provide between 500 and 1000 gigawatts

insights.claim: These technologies complement variable wind and solar power by providing low-emissions electricity that is always on or fills generation gaps, require less land in densely populated high-emissions-growth areas, and can supply heat for buildings and factories that renewables cannot.
insights.speaker: David M. Hart
insights.so_what: Articulates the demand-side complementarity thesis (dispatchable clean power plus industrial heat) underlying why these technologies scale on a distinct addressable market from renewables, informing clean-tech sector allocation.
insights.locator: The global impact of these technologies could be immense

insights.claim: Enhanced-geothermal leader Fervo raised billions of dollars in a May 2026 IPO and its filing suggests plans to build eighty to one hundred commercial-scale power plants; advanced-nuclear firms Kairos, Oklo, and X-Energy are raising capital, breaking ground, and signing deals to sell energy; and Form Energy (iron-air long-duration batteries) and Antora Energy (thermal/heat batteries) have ramped up production, all under OB3's preserved tax credits.
insights.speaker: David M. Hart
insights.so_what: A direct, named watchlist of companies showing real capital-raising and deployment momentum under OB3's preserved credits, relevant for investors positioned in immature clean-technology plays.
insights.locator: OB3 preserved tax credits for these and other less mature clean energy technologies

insights.claim: Hart frames OB3's clean-tech provisions as a potential 'counterweight to China's dominance of today's clean energy supply chains,' offering durable industrial-policy continuity for these technologies that could outlast both the Biden and Trump administrations.
insights.speaker: David M. Hart
insights.so_what: Frames advanced nuclear, geothermal, and storage as a geopolitical and industrial-policy play against Chinese clean-tech supply-chain dominance, a lens for evaluating the bipartisan durability of these subsidies.
insights.quote: counterweight to China's dominance of today's clean energy supply chains
insights.locator: providing a foundation that their successors can build upon
